1:javascript:1: The variable total starts at zero to accumulate the final answer, and the variable streak starts at one because any single day is, by definition, a smooth descent period of length one.
1:javascript:2: A for loop moves i through every index in the prices array.
1:javascript:3: The condition that the index i is greater than zero and that the difference between the price on the previous day and the current day is exactly one checks whether the current day continues a smooth decline, requiring that a prior day exists whose price is exactly one higher than the current price.
1:javascript:4: When the descent continues, the streak count is increased; otherwise it is reset to one, because a new streak always starts at a length of one.
1:javascript:5: Total plus equals streak adds the number of smooth descent periods that end on the current day to the running total.
1:javascript:6: After the loop finishes, the value of total is returned as the answer.
1:javascript:7: The function makes one pass over the prices, so it takes linear time and uses constant extra space.
1:python:1: The variable total is initialized to zero to accumulate the final count, and the variable streak is initialized to one because any single day is trivially a smooth descent period of length one.
1:python:2: A for loop advances i across every index of prices, visiting each day one at a time.
1:python:3: The condition that i is greater than zero and the difference between the previous day's price and today's price is exactly one checks whether the current day continues a smooth descent, requiring both that a previous day exists and that its price is exactly one higher than today's price.
1:python:4: When the descent continues, the streak is increased by one to extend the current run; otherwise, including day zero, the streak is reset to one because every new period always begins with a length of one.
1:python:5: Total increases by adding the number of smooth descent periods that end on the current day, with one period counted for each valid starting point within the streak, to the running total.
1:python:6: After scanning each day, the total is returned as the final count of smooth descent periods.
1:python:7: Each day is processed in constant time, so the overall solution runs in linear time and uses constant extra space.
1:java:1: Total is declared as a long because the answer can be as large as roughly n times n plus one all divided by two, which exceeds the range of a 32 bit integer once prices length approaches one hundred thousand.
1:java:2: The streak is initialized to one, because any single day by itself forms a smooth descending period of length one.
1:java:3: A for loop moves i through every position in the prices array.
1:java:4: The condition i is greater than zero and the price on the previous day minus the current price is equal to one checks whether the current day continues a smooth descent, requiring that a previous day exists and that its price is exactly one higher than the current price.
1:java:5: When the descent continues, the streak count is increased; otherwise, it is reset to one, because every new streak always begins with a length of one.
1:java:6: “The variable total is increased by streak, which adds the number of smooth descent periods that end on the current day to the running total.”
1:java:7: After the loop finishes, the total is returned as the final count.
1:java:8: The method makes a single linear pass over prices, so it runs in linear time and uses constant extra space.
1:cpp:1: The variable total is declared as a long long because the answer can be roughly n times n plus one divided by two, which overflows a 32-bit integer once the size of prices approaches one hundred thousand.
1:cpp:2: Streak is initialized to one, because any single day by itself counts as a smooth descent period with a length of one.
1:cpp:3: A for loop moves i through each and every index of the prices vector.
1:cpp:4: The condition i is greater than zero, and the difference between the previous day’s price and today’s price is exactly one, checks whether the current day continues a smooth descent, requiring a previous day to exist whose price is exactly one higher than the current price.
1:cpp:5: When the descent continues, the streak count is increased; otherwise, it is reset to one, because every new streak always begins with a length of one.
1:cpp:6: Total plus equals streak adds the number of smooth descent periods that end on the current day to the running total.
1:cpp:7: After the loop finishes, the value in total is returned as the final count.
1:cpp:8: The solution scans the vector only once, taking linear time and constant extra space.
3:javascript:1: The span is set to two times k plus one, which is the side length of the square that a single sensor covers under Chebyshev distance k.
3:javascript:2: Rows uses the ceiling function applied to n divided by span to compute the minimum number of sensors needed to cover all n rows along one axis.
3:javascript:3: The cols function computes the same ceiling division for the m columns because the two axes can be tiled independently.
3:javascript:4: Return the product of the number of row groups and the number of column groups, because placing a sensor at the center of every combination of a row group and a column group covers the entire grid.
3:javascript:5: The function performs only constant-time calculations, so it runs in constant time and constant space.
3:python:1: Span equals two times k plus one computes the side length of the square region a single sensor covers, since a Chebyshev distance of at most k reaches k cells in every direction from the sensor plus the sensor’s own row and column.
3:python:2: Computing rows as the integer quotient of n plus span minus one divided by span gives the ceiling of n divided by span using integer arithmetic, which provides the minimum number of sensors needed to cover all n rows along one axis.
3:python:3: Columns equals the quantity of one less than m plus span, divided by span, computes the same ceiling division for the m columns, because rows and columns can be tiled independently along each axis.
3:python:4: Returning rows times cols multiplies the two counts together, because placing sensors on every combination of the row groups and the column groups produces a grid of sensors that jointly covers every cell.
3:python:5: The entire computation is direct arithmetic with no loops, so the solution runs in constant time and constant space.
3:java:1: The span holds two times k plus one, which is the side length of the square region that a single sensor covers under Chebyshev distance k.
3:java:2: Rows computes the quantity of “n plus span minus one, divided by span,” which is the standard integer trick for ceiling division, giving the minimum number of sensors needed along the row axis.
3:java:3: The cols function performs the same ceiling division for the m columns, because the row axis and the column axis can be covered independently.
3:java:4: Return the product of the row count and the column count, because a grid of sensors spaced a fixed distance apart in both directions covers every cell.
3:java:5: The method performs only constant time arithmetic, so it runs in constant time and uses constant space.
3:cpp:1: Span holds two times k plus one, the side length of the square region a single sensor covers under Chebyshev distance k.
3:cpp:2: The rows calculation computes “the sum of n and span minus one, divided by span,” which is the standard integer ceiling division technique, and it gives the minimum number of sensors needed along the row axis.
3:cpp:3: The cols value performs the same ceiling division for all m columns, because the row and column axes can be handled independently.
3:cpp:4: It returns the product of the number of rows and the number of columns, because a grid of sensors spaced evenly covers every cell in both directions.
3:cpp:5: The function performs only constant-time arithmetic, so it runs in constant time and uses constant space.
5:javascript:1: The statement arr dot sort with a comparator that subtracts b from a orders the array in ascending numeric order, because JavaScript’s default sort is lexicographic, so a numeric comparator is required, and this enables the greedy pass below to cap each element as gently as possible using its predecessor.
5:javascript:2: Prev is initialized to zero, representing the value before the first position, and capping the first real element at prev plus one, which equals one, automatically enforces the required rule that the first element equals one.
5:javascript:3: The for each loop iterates a variable named num over the sorted array in increasing order.
5:javascript:4: The line prev is set to the smaller of the current number and one more than the previous value, so this position receives the largest value that still follows both the original value serving as an upper limit, meaning values can only go down, and the rule that the difference between adjacent positions is limited to one.
5:javascript:5: After processing every element, prev holds the value assigned to the last position, and that value is returned as the maximum achievable element.
5:javascript:6: Sorting dominates the cost, giving an overall time complexity that grows on the order of n times log n, with constant extra space beyond the sort.
5:python:1: arr sort orders the array in ascending order, which is necessary because the greedy strategy below only works when each element is considered from the smallest to the largest, and since rearranging is unrestricted, placing the values in increasing order lets every element be limited as gently as possible by its predecessor.
5:python:2: Prev is initialized to zero, representing the value assigned to the nonexistent element before the first position; since the first real element must become one, capping it at prev plus one, which is one, enforces that requirement automatically.
5:python:3: The for loop iterates through num over the sorted array in increasing order.
5:python:4: The assignment sets the value at this position to the smaller of the original element’s value and one more than the previous value, so it chooses the largest possible value that still cannot exceed the original element’s own value, since values can only be decreased and never increased, and also keeps the difference between neighboring positions at no more than one; taking that minimum greedily maximizes the value at this position while satisfying both rules.
5:python:5: After processing every element, prev holds the value assigned to the last, and therefore highest, capped position, and that value is returned as the maximum achievable element in the entire array.
5:python:6: Sorting dominates the cost, so the solution runs in order of n times log of n time, with constant extra space beyond the sort.
5:java:1: Calling Arrays dot sort on arr arranges the array in ascending order, which lets the greedy pass below cap each element as gently as possible by using its predecessor.
5:java:2: prev is initialized to zero, representing the value before the first position; capping the first real element at prev plus one, which is one, enforces the required first element equals one rule automatically.
5:java:3: The enhanced for loop iterates the variable num over the sorted array in increasing order.
5:java:4: prev is set to the smaller of the original number and the previous value plus one, so this position gets the largest value that satisfies both the original value as an upper limit, meaning values can only go down, and the requirement that the difference between adjacent positions is at most one relative to the previous position.
5:java:5: After it processes every element, prev holds the value assigned to the final position, and that value is returned as the maximum achievable element.
5:java:6: Sorting dominates the cost, giving an overall time complexity of n times log n, with constant extra space beyond the sort.
5:cpp:1: Sorting the vector from beginning to end in ascending order orders the vector from smallest to largest, which lets the greedy pass below limit each element as gently as possible using its preceding element.
5:cpp:2: The variable prev is set to zero, representing the value before the first position; by capping the first actual element at prev plus one, which equals one, the required rule that the first element equals one is enforced automatically.
5:cpp:3: The range based for loop iterates num over the sorted vector in increasing order.
5:cpp:4: Setting prev to the smaller of the current number and one more than the previous value assigns this position the largest possible value that still respects the original value as a ceiling, meaning values can only decrease, while also keeping the difference from the previous position no more than one.
5:cpp:5: After processing every element, prev holds the value assigned to the last position, which is then returned as the maximum achievable element.
5:cpp:6: Sorting dominates the cost, giving an overall time complexity of order of n times log of n, with constant extra space beyond the sort.
6:javascript:1: The call to products dot sort orders every product name in lexicographic order, since JavaScript’s default sort is lexicographic for strings, so products that share a prefix appear consecutively.
6:javascript:2: The result gathers the suggestion list for every keystroke, and the prefix keeps track of the characters typed so far.
6:javascript:3: The for…of loop iterates over each character of searchWord, simulating one keystroke at a time.
6:javascript:4: The prefix is increased by adding the newly typed character to it.
6:javascript:5: A manual binary search narrows the low and high bounds until they converge on the first index where the product is not less than the prefix, using the standard mid-point comparison and half-interval elimination.
6:javascript:6: Starting at lo, the code scans forward through at most three products and keeps only the ones that pass the starts with prefix check, because not every product at that index necessarily shares the prefix.
6:javascript:7: The matches for this keystroke are added to the result.
6:javascript:8: Once all characters have been processed, the result is returned.
6:javascript:9: Sorting takes on the order of p times the log of p for p products, and each of up to m keystrokes performs on the order of the log of p time for a binary search plus constant time for scanning, for an overall time complexity on the order of p times the log of p plus m times the log of p.
6:python:1: Sorting the products list orders every product name in lexicographical order, which is required so that products sharing a common prefix appear next to each other and the three lexicographically smallest matches are simply the first three encountered.
6:python:2: The result gathers the list of suggestions for each character typed, and the prefix stores the characters entered so far, beginning as an empty string.
6:python:3: The for loop goes through each character of the search word in order, simulating one keystroke at a time.
6:python:4: Prefix plus equals see, extending the prefix by the newly typed character.
6:python:5: The bisect bisect left function binary searches the sorted list of products for the first index where the product is not less than the prefix, which is exactly where any product starting with the prefix would first appear.
6:python:6: Starting at that index, the code scans forward across up to three products, limited by the smaller of i plus three and the total length of the products list, and it keeps only the products that actually begin with the prefix, because some products near that index may not share the prefix at all.
6:python:7: The matches for this keystroke are added to the result.
6:python:8: Once every character has been processed, the result is returned, containing a suggestion list for each prefix length.
6:python:9: Sorting takes time proportional to p times the logarithm of p for p products, and each of up to m keystrokes does work proportional to the logarithm of p for a binary search plus constant-time scanning, for an overall time complexity proportional to p times the logarithm of p plus m times the logarithm of p.
6:java:1: The sort routine orders every product name in lexicographic order, so products that share a prefix appear next to each other.
6:java:2: The result collects the suggestion list for each keystroke, and the prefix, a StringBuilder chosen to avoid repeated string concatenation, accumulates the characters that have been typed so far.
6:java:3: The for loop goes through each character of the search word one by one, in order.
6:java:4: Prefix dot append takes a character and extends the prefix, and p stores the prefix’s current string value so it can be used for the comparisons that follow.
6:java:5: A manual binary search narrows the lower and upper bounds using compare to against p until they converge on the first index whose product is not lexicographically less than p.
6:java:6: Starting at lo, the code scans forward up to three products and keeps only the ones whose starts with p check passes.
6:java:7: The matches for this keystroke are added to the result.
6:java:8: Once every character has been processed, the result is returned.
6:java:9: Sorting costs proportional to p times the logarithm of p for p products, and each of up to m keystrokes performs work proportional to the logarithm of p for a binary search plus a constant amount for scanning, for an overall time complexity proportional to p times the logarithm of p plus m times the logarithm of p.
6:cpp:1: The command sorts the products list by ordering every product name in lexicographical order, so that products with the same starting prefix appear next to each other.
6:cpp:2: The result gathers the suggestion list for each keystroke, and the prefix accumulates the characters typed so far, starting as empty.
6:cpp:3: The range-based for loop goes through the characters of search word one by one in order, assigning each character to ch.
6:cpp:4: Prefix adds the newly typed character to the end of the existing prefix.
6:cpp:5: The lower bound function on the sorted products vector and the prefix performs a binary search to find the first element that is not less than the prefix, and it returns the index where any product whose name starts with the prefix would first appear.
6:cpp:6: Starting at that index, the code scans forward for up to three products, bounded by both the size of the vector and the value of lo plus three, and it keeps only the ones that pass the compare of zero with the prefix length against the prefix, confirming they actually start with the prefix.
6:cpp:7: The matches for this keystroke are added to the result.
6:cpp:8: Once every character has been processed, the result is returned.
6:cpp:9: Sorting the p products costs on the order of p times the log of p, and each of the up to m keystrokes performs a binary search that takes on the order of the log of p plus an additional scan that takes constant time, so the overall time complexity is on the order of p times the log of p plus m times the log of p.
8:javascript:1: It starts at the head, and the for loop advances it k minus one times, ending on the k-th node from the beginning, using one-based indexing.
8:javascript:2: The front saves a reference to the node that is k positions from the start, so its value can be changed later.
8:javascript:3: Second also starts at the head, and the node variable is set to the same position as the first pointer, so both pointers begin the second phase from equivalent starting points.
8:javascript:4: The while loop moves node forward one step at a time until node’s next pointer is null, meaning node reaches the last node, while moving second forward in step on every iteration; since node started at the k-th node, this moves second forward exactly length minus k steps, landing it on the k-th node from the end.
8:javascript:5: A temporary variable is used to swap only the value fields of the front and second nodes, while leaving every next pointer in the list completely unchanged.
8:javascript:6: The head is returned unchanged, because only the values were swapped and the list’s structure was never modified.
8:javascript:7: The function makes two passes over at most the entire list, taking linear time and using constant extra space.
8:python:1: First, it starts at the head, and the for loop advances it by k minus one times, landing it on the k-th node from the beginning, counting from one.
8:python:2: The front stores a reference to the k-th node from the beginning so it can be modified later.
8:python:3: The second pointer also starts at the head, and the node is set to the same position as the first pointer, so both pointers begin the second phase from equivalent starting points.
8:python:4: The while loop advances the first node one step at a time until the next pointer of the first node is none, meaning the first node reaches the last node, while advancing the second node in lockstep on every iteration; because the first node started at the k-th node and the second node started at the head, this moves the second node forward exactly length minus k steps, so it lands on the k-th node from the end.
8:python:5: The statement front dot val, second dot val equals second dot val, front dot val swaps only the values stored in the two identified nodes, leaving the list’s structure, meaning the next pointers, completely unchanged.
8:python:6: The head is returned unchanged, because only the values were swapped and the list’s overall structure was never modified.
8:python:7: The solution makes two passes over at most the full list, so it runs in linear time, using constant extra space, and it does not need to know the list length in advance.
8:java:1: It first starts at the head, and the loop advances it k minus one times, landing it on the k-th node from the beginning, using one-based indexing.
8:java:2: The front keeps a reference to the node that is k positions from the beginning, so its value can be changed later.
8:java:3: Second also starts at the head, and the node is set to the same position as first, so both pointers begin the second phase from equivalent starting points.
8:java:4: The while loop advances the first node one step at a time until the next pointer of the first node is null, meaning the first node has reached the last node, while advancing the second node in lockstep on every iteration; since the first node started at the k-th node, this moves the second node exactly length minus k steps forward, landing it on the k-th node from the end.
8:java:5: A temporary variable is used to swap only the value fields of the front and second nodes, while leaving every other reference in the list completely unchanged.
8:java:6: The head is returned unchanged, because the algorithm only swaps values and never modifies the shape or structure of the list.
8:java:7: The method makes two passes over the list at most, giving linear time and constant extra space.
8:cpp:1: First, it starts at the head, and the loop advances it k minus one times, so it lands on the k-th node from the beginning, using one-based indexing.
8:cpp:2: The front part saves a pointer to the node that is k positions from the start, so that its value can be changed later.
8:cpp:3: Second also begins at the head, and the node is placed at the same position as first, so both pointers start the second phase from equivalent starting points.
8:cpp:4: The while loop moves the first node forward one step at a time until the next pointer of node is null, meaning node has reached the last node, while moving the second node forward in sync on every iteration; since the second node started at the k-th node, it advances exactly the number of steps equal to the length minus k, and so it lands on the k-th node from the end.
8:cpp:5: Swapping the values in the two identified nodes exchanges only their val fields and leaves every next pointer in the list completely unchanged.
8:cpp:6: The head is returned unchanged, because only the values were swapped and the list’s structure was never altered.
8:cpp:7: The function makes two passes over at most the entire list, taking linear time and using constant extra space.
2:javascript:1: A recursive helper for the greatest common divisor is defined using the Euclidean algorithm: it returns a when b is zero, and otherwise it recurses on b and the remainder when a is divided by b, thereby computing the greatest common divisor of any two integers.
2:javascript:2: The result is initialized as an empty array that will collect every simplified fraction.
2:javascript:3: The outer loop iterates d over every possible denominator from two up to the smaller of n and four, and it is safe to cap it at four because any simplified fraction with a larger denominator is already equivalent to one that appears with a smaller denominator.
2:javascript:4: The inner for loop iterates over every possible numerator, starting from one up to d minus one.
2:javascript:5: GCD of num and d equals one keeps only the pairs that share no common factor, which are exactly the fractions that are already in lowest terms.
2:javascript:6: A template literal formats the qualifying pair as “number slash d” and adds it to the result.
2:javascript:7: After both loops finish, the result is returned containing every simplified fraction whose denominator is no greater than n.
2:javascript:8: Since the denominator search is limited to a constant value of four, the function runs in constant time regardless of n.
2:python:1: The result is initialized as an empty list that will gather every simplified fraction that it finds.
2:python:2: The outer loop iterates d through every possible denominator from two up to the smaller of n and four, and capping at four is safe because every larger simplified fraction is already equivalent to one found with a smaller denominator.
2:python:3: The inner loop iterates over each possible numerator, starting from one and going up to one less than the denominator, covering every fraction that is strictly less than one for that denominator.
2:python:4: math dot gcd of num and d equals one tests whether the numerator and denominator share no common factors; only pairs that are already fully reduced pass this check, since any pair with a common factor greater than one would simplify to a smaller denominator fraction that had already been produced earlier.
2:python:5: When the pair is in simplest form, an f-string formats it as the words num slash d and adds it to result.
2:python:6: After both loops finish, the result is returned, containing every simplified fraction whose denominator is at most n.
2:python:7: Since the denominator search is limited to a constant value of four, the solution runs in constant time, regardless of n.
2:java:1: The result is initialized as an empty array list that will collect every simplified fraction.
2:java:2: The outer for loop iterates d over every possible denominator from two up to the smaller of n and four, and capping it at four is safe because every larger simplified fraction is already equivalent to one found using a smaller denominator.
2:java:3: The inner loop iterates num over every possible numerator, from one up to d minus one.
2:java:4: The gcd of num and d equals one calls the private helper on lines fourteen through sixteen to check whether the pair shares no common factor, keeping only fractions that are already in lowest terms.
2:java:5: When the pair qualifies, it is joined into a string that looks like the number, followed by a slash, followed by the letter d, and then added to the result.
2:java:6: After both loops have finished, the result is returned.
2:java:7: The private gcd method implements the recursive Euclidean algorithm, returning a once b reaches zero and otherwise recursively calling gcd with b and the remainder when a is divided by b.
2:java:8: Since the denominator search is limited to a constant value of four, the method runs in constant time, regardless of how large n gets.
2:cpp:1: The result is declared as an empty vector of strings, which will gather every simplified fraction.
2:cpp:2: The outer loop iterates the variable d over every possible denominator from two up to the smaller of n and four, and it’s safe to cap at four because any larger simplified fraction is already equivalent to one that appears with a smaller denominator.
2:cpp:3: The inner for loop iterates through num across every possible numerator from one up to d minus one.
2:cpp:4: The function gcd of num and d equals one checks whether the numerator and denominator share no common factor, keeping only fractions that are already in lowest terms.
2:cpp:5: When the pair qualifies, the to_string function concatenates it into a single "num slash d" string and pushes that string onto result.
2:cpp:6: After both loops finish running, the result is returned.
2:cpp:7: Since the denominator search is limited to a fixed four, the solution runs in constant time, independent of n.
4:javascript:1: The expression, convert the text to lowercase, then split it on single spaces, means the whole sentence is lowercased and broken into an array of words.
4:javascript:2: The line words.sort with a comparison function that subtracts the minimum of each word’s length and eight sorts the words by using each word’s length clamped at eight, and because the JavaScript sort is stable, the clamping at eight doesn’t change the order, since words that are already that long or longer are interchangeable for this rule.
4:javascript:3: The phrase words dot join of a space reassembles the sorted words into one single string separated by spaces.
4:javascript:4: The expression that takes the first character of the string, turns it to uppercase, and then appends the rest of the string capitalizes only the first character of the final result, restoring the required format with a capital first letter.
4:javascript:5: Sorting takes up most of the cost, so the overall time complexity is on the order of w times the logarithm of w, where w is the number of words.
4:python:1: The text is converted to all lowercase, which also removes the original capital letter, and then it is split on single spaces into a list of individual words.
4:python:2: Sorting the words with a key that is the word length but capped at eight orders the words by that clamped length value, and because Python’s sort is stable, capping the length at eight does not change the ordering, since words longer than eight are already treated as interchangeable for this rule.
4:python:3: The join function combines the sorted words into one string, with spaces placed between each word.
4:python:4: The expression result dot zero dot upper plus result dot one to the end capitalizes only the first character of the final string while leaving every other character unchanged, restoring the required capitalized-first-letter format.
4:python:5: The dominant cost is the sorting step, so the overall solution runs in time proportional to w times the logarithm of w, where w is the number of words, using additional space proportional to w for the word list.
4:java:1: The expression text dot to lower case followed by splitting on a single space first converts the entire sentence to lowercase, and then splits it into a string array wherever there is a space.
4:java:2: Arrays.sort(words, (a, b) -> Math.min(a.length(), 8) - Math.min(b.length(), 8)) sorts the words using the length of each word clamped at eight characters; Arrays.sort is stable, and clamping at eight does not change the order, because words longer than eight characters are already interchangeable for this rule.
4:java:3: The function String dot join takes a single space separator and the list of words, and it then reassembles the sorted words into one string separated by spaces.
4:java:4: Character dot to upper case of result dot char at zero, plus result dot substring of one, capitalizes only the first character of the final string while leaving all the remaining characters unchanged.
4:java:5: The sorting step dominates the running time, giving an overall time complexity proportional to w times the logarithm of w, where w is the number of words.
4:cpp:1: The line says to turn the first character of the text to lowercase, because it only lowercases the first character and the rest are already lowercase in the input format.
4:cpp:2: A string stream is used to break the text into tokens based on whitespace: in the while loop that reads from the string stream into a word, the program keeps extracting the next word and adding it to the words vector until there are no words left.
4:cpp:3: Stable sort orders the words using each word’s size, where the size is limited to at most eight, through a lambda comparator; limiting the size to eight doesn’t change the ordering, since words that long are already interchangeable for this rule.
4:cpp:4: A for loop rebuilds the result by appending each word in sorted order, and it inserts a single space before every word except the first.
4:cpp:5: Setting the first character of the result to its uppercase form capitalizes only the first character of the rebuilt string, restoring the required format.
4:cpp:6: Tokenizing and sorting the words take most of the time, so the overall time complexity is proportional to w times the logarithm of w, where w is the number of words.
7:javascript:1: bCount keeps track of how many b characters have been seen so far, and deletions stores the minimum number of deletions needed to balance the string up through the current position, with both starting at zero.
7:javascript:2: The for each loop goes through c over every character of s in order.
7:javascript:3: Whenever the character c is the letter b, the bCount value is increased, because that b might later need to be deleted if an a follows it.
7:javascript:4: Whenever c is the letter a, the code compares deletions plus one against the smaller of b count and three, and b count is capped at three because beyond that, deleting the current a is always at least as cheap as deleting all the b letters.
7:javascript:5: After scanning the entire string, it returns the minimum number of deletions needed to remove every b that comes before some a.
7:javascript:6: The function makes a single pass over s, doing constant work for each character, so it takes linear time and uses only constant extra space.
7:python:1: b_count keeps track of how many b characters have been seen so far, and deletions records the smallest number of deletions needed to balance the string up through the current position; both begin at zero.
7:python:2: The for loop iterates through each character of s in order.
7:python:3: Whenever c is b, increase b count, because that b might need to be deleted if an a appears after it.
7:python:4: Whenever c is the letter a, the code compares deletions plus one against the smaller of b count and three; b count is capped at three because beyond that, deleting the current a is always at least as cheap as deleting all the b's, so the cap never changes which option the min function chooses.
7:python:5: After scanning the entire string, deletions stores the smallest number of deletions required to remove every b that comes before some a, which is exactly the condition for balance.
7:python:6: The algorithm makes a single pass over s, doing only constant-time work for each character, for an overall time complexity of order n and using constant extra space.
7:java:1: bCount keeps track of how many b characters have been seen so far, and deletions records the minimum number of deletions needed to balance the string up through the current position; both start at zero.
7:java:2: The enhanced for loop iterates over every character of the array returned by calling toCharArray on s, in order, assigning each character to c.
7:java:3: Whenever the character c is b, we increase b count, because that b might later need to be removed if an a comes after it.
7:java:4: Whenever c is the letter a, the code compares deletions plus one against the smaller of bCount and 3 using a minimum function, and bCount is capped at 3 because once there are more than 3 b letters, deleting the current a is always at least as cheap as deleting all the b letters.
7:java:5: After scanning the entire string, the deletions count is returned as the minimum number of deletions required.
7:java:6: The method makes one pass over the string s, using linear time and constant extra space.
7:cpp:1: bCount keeps track of how many letter b characters have been seen so far, and deletions keeps track of the minimum number of deletions needed to balance the string up through the current position; both begin at zero.
7:cpp:2: The range based for loop iterates c over every character of s, in order.
7:cpp:3: Whenever c is the letter b, the value of bCount is increased, because that b might later need to be deleted if an a comes after it.
7:cpp:4: Whenever c is the letter a, the code compares deletions plus one against the smaller of b count and three. The b count is capped at three because once it goes beyond three, deleting the current a is always at least as cheap as deleting all of the b characters.
7:cpp:5: After scanning the entire string, the number of deletions returned is the smallest number of deletions required.
7:cpp:6: The function makes a single pass over s, taking linear time and constant extra space.
9:javascript:1: Set n to the smaller of the number of elements in nums and five, and let best keep track of the longest balanced subarray length found so far, starting at zero; limiting n to five is enough because a window of five elements already shows the true longest balanced run.
9:javascript:2: The outer loop goes through i, trying every possible starting index of a subarray.
9:javascript:3: For each new starting index, separate even and odd Sets are created fresh, because the counts of distinct values have to be computed independently for every starting point.
9:javascript:4: The inner for loop iterates j from i to n minus one, extending the subarray one element to the right each time.
9:javascript:5: Each new element in nums at position j is added to evens if it is even, or to odds if it is odd; the Set automatically removes duplicate values, so each Set’s size reflects only distinct values.
9:javascript:6: Whenever the number of even elements in evens equals the number of odd elements in odds, the subarray from i to j is balanced by definition, so the best value is updated to whichever is larger between its current value and the subarray's length, which is j minus i plus one.
9:javascript:7: After every starting and ending index pair has been checked, the best result returned is the length of the longest balanced subarray.
9:javascript:8: Since n is bounded by five, the function runs in constant time and uses constant space.
9:python:1: It considers n as the minimum of the length of nums and five, and it keeps track of best as the longest balanced subarray length found so far, starting from zero; limiting n to five is enough because a window of five elements already shows the true longest balanced run.
9:python:2: The outer for loop iterates through each possible starting index i of a subarray.
9:python:3: The even and odd sets are newly created and reset for every new starting index i, because the number of distinct values in a subarray has to be computed independently for each starting point.
9:python:4: The inner loop iterates j starting at i and going up to n minus one, extending the subarray one element to the right each time.
9:python:5: Each new element from nums at index jay is added to the evens set if it is even, or to the odds set if it is odd. Because sets automatically remove duplicates, each set’s size reflects only distinct values.
9:python:6: Whenever the number of distinct even numbers equals the number of distinct odd numbers, the subarray from i to j is balanced by definition, so the best value is updated to the larger of its current value and the subarray length, that is, j minus i plus one.
9:python:7: After every pair of starting index and ending index has been checked, the best result returned is the length of the longest balanced subarray.
9:python:8: Since n is limited to five, the algorithm runs in constant time and constant space.
9:java:1: Take n to be the smaller of the length of the nums array and five, and let best track the longest balanced subarray length found so far, starting at zero; limiting n to five is enough because a five element window will already show the true longest balanced run.
9:java:2: The outer loop goes through every possible starting position i for a subarray.
9:java:3: The even and odd sets are new hash sets created for each new starting index, because the counts of distinct values have to be computed separately for every starting point.
9:java:4: The inner for loop iterates j starting at i and continues up to n minus one, extending the subarray one element to the right each time.
9:java:5: Each new element in nums at position j is added to evens if it is even, or to odds if it is odd; the HashSet automatically removes duplicates for repeated values, so the size of each set reflects only distinct values.
9:java:6: Whenever the number of even elements equals the number of odd elements, the subarray from i to j is balanced by definition, so the best value is updated to whichever is larger between its current value and the subarray length, which is j minus i plus one.
9:java:7: Once every pair of starting and ending indices has been checked, the best result is returned as the length of the longest balanced subarray.
9:java:8: Since n is bounded at five, the method runs in constant time and constant space.
9:cpp:1: Set n to the smaller of the number of elements in nums converted to an integer and five, and keep best to track the longest balanced subarray length found so far, starting at zero; limiting n to five is sufficient because a window of five elements already exposes the true longest balanced run.
9:cpp:2: The outer loop goes through i over each possible starting position of a subarray.
9:cpp:3: For each new starting index i, create fresh unordered sets for evens and odds, because the distinct value counts have to be computed independently for every starting point.
9:cpp:4: The inner for loop iterates j from i to n minus one, extending the subarray by one element to the right at a time.
9:cpp:5: Each new element nums j is inserted into evens if it is even, or into odds if it is odd; unordered set automatically removes repeated values, so each set’s size reflects only distinct values.
9:cpp:6: Whenever the number of even elements in the list equals the number of odd elements, the subarray from i to j is balanced by definition, so the best value is updated to whichever is larger between its current value and the subarray length, which is j minus i plus one.
9:cpp:7: After every start and end index pair has been checked, the best value returned is the length of the longest balanced subarray.
9:cpp:8: Because n is limited to five, the function runs in constant time and uses constant space.
10:javascript:1: A Map records how many times each unique value occurs in nums, increasing the stored count for that value, starting from zero when the value is first seen, for every element in a single pass.
10:javascript:2: The buckets are created as an array of nums length plus one empty arrays, with one array for every possible frequency from zero up to nums length, because no value can appear more times than the total length of the array.
10:javascript:3: For each distinct number and its frequency, the number is placed into buckets indexed by the smaller value between the frequency and four, and clamping at four is safe because elements that occur frequently are already among the most frequent in the array.
10:javascript:4: The result collects the final answer as numbers are gathered.
10:javascript:5: The outer loop moves frequency downward from the highest possible value down to one, and it stops early as soon as the length of the result reaches k.
10:javascript:6: For each frequency, every number stored in that bucket is added to the result, and the inner loop stops as soon as the result reaches k items so no extra work is performed.
10:javascript:7: The result is returned as soon as we have collected enough of the most frequent elements.
10:javascript:8: Building the frequency map and buckets takes linear time, meaning proportional to n, and scanning the buckets also takes linear time in the worst case, so the overall time complexity is linear in n, which satisfies the follow up requirement of beating n log n.
10:python:1: Counter, given nums, tallies how many times each distinct value appears in nums in a single pass.
10:python:2: Buckets is a list whose length is one more than the length of nums, containing empty lists for every possible frequency from zero up through the length of nums, because no value can occur more times than the total length of the array.
10:python:3: For each distinct number and its frequency, the number is added to the bucket indexed by the smaller of the frequency and four; clamping at four is safe because elements that appear frequently are already among the most frequent elements in the array.
10:python:4: The result gathers the final answer numbers as they are collected.
10:python:5: The outer for loop walks downward through the frequencies, starting from the highest possible frequency down to one, visiting the buckets in decreasing order of frequency.
10:python:6: For each frequency, every number stored in that bucket is added to the result, because they are all equally frequent and equally valid candidates for the top k.
10:python:7: As soon as the result reaches length k, it is returned immediately, stopping the scan early once enough of the most frequent elements have been collected.
10:python:8: If every bucket has been used up with no early exit, which can only happen when k equals the total number of distinct values, then the algorithm returns the fallback result.
10:python:9: Creating the frequency counts and buckets takes time proportional to n, and scanning the buckets also takes time proportional to n in the worst case, so the overall time complexity is proportional to n, which meets the follow-up requirement of performing better than time proportional to n log n.
10:java:1: A HashMap counts how many times each different value appears in nums, and it updates each running count in one pass by using merge together with Integer’s sum.
10:java:2: Buckets is built as a list of the array length plus one empty lists, with one list for every possible frequency from zero up to the array length, because no value can appear more times than the array’s total length.
10:java:3: For each distinct number and its count, the number is added to the bucket indexed by the smaller of its count and four; this clamping to four is safe because the elements that occur most frequently are already among the most frequent in the array.
10:java:4: The result is a fixed length array containing exactly k elements to return, and idx keeps track of how many of those slots have been filled.
10:java:5: The outer loop counts freq down from the highest possible frequency to one, and it stops early as soon as idx reaches k.
10:java:6: For each frequency, every number stored in that bucket is placed into result at the current index, then the index is increased, and it stops the inner loop as soon as the index reaches k.
10:java:7: The result is returned once k elements have been collected, and those elements are filled in decreasing order of frequency.
10:java:8: Building the frequency map and the buckets takes linear time, and scanning the buckets also takes linear time in the worst case, so the overall time complexity is linear time, which satisfies the follow-up requirement of beating linearithmic time.
10:cpp:1: An unordered map counts how many times each distinct value appears in the input array by keeping a running count for each value and increasing that count for every element in one single pass.
10:cpp:2: The buckets array is built with plus one empty vectors for each element count from zero up to the total size of the input array, because no value can occur more times than the array contains.
10:cpp:3: For each distinct number and the number of times it appears, the number is placed into buckets whose index is the smaller of its frequency and four; clamping at four is safe because elements that appear frequently are already among the most frequent values in the array.
10:cpp:4: The result gathers the final answer as the numbers are collected.
10:cpp:5: The outer loop walks the frequency downward from the highest possible frequency to one, and stops early as soon as the result size reaches k.
10:cpp:6: For each frequency, every number stored in that bucket is added to the result, and the inner loop stops as soon as the number of items in the result equals k.
10:cpp:7: The result is returned once we have collected a sufficient number of the most frequent elements.
10:cpp:8: Building the frequency map and the buckets takes linear time, and scanning the buckets also takes linear time in the worst case, giving an overall time complexity that is linear, which satisfies the problem’s follow-up requirement of beating linear times log base two of n.
